---
title: Syllabus
---

:::: {.grid .course-details}

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-6 .g-col-md-4}
### Instructor

- {{< fa user >}} [{{< var instructor.name >}}]({{< var instructor.url >}})
- {{< fa building >}} {{< var instructor.office >}}
- {{< fa envelope >}} [{{< var instructor.email >}}](mailto:{{< var instructor.email >}})
- {{< fa clock >}} {{< var instructor.office_hours >}}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-sm-6 .g-col-md-4}
### Course Details

- {{< fa calendar >}} {{< var course.semester >}}
- {{< fa calendar-days >}} {{< var course.dates >}}
- {{< fa location-dot >}} {{< var course.location >}}
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-4}
### Contact Policy

{{< var instructor.contact_policy >}}

[{{< fa calendar-check >}} Schedule an appointment]({{< var instructor.appointment_url >}})
:::

::::


## Course Description

**{{< var course.number >}}: {{< var course.title >}}** is a graduate-level course that develops practical competence in regression-based data analysis using R. This course follows a **"specific to general" pedagogical approach**, beginning with simple linear regression---a model most students have encountered---and progressively extending to more complex frameworks.

### Learning Arc

We begin with **Simple Linear Regression** and expand to:

- **ANCOVA and ANOVA** --- regression with categorical predictors
- **Multiple Regression** --- adding continuous predictors and understanding confounding
- **Interactions and Nonlinear Relationships** --- capturing complexity in data
- **Model Building and Diagnostics** --- ensuring model validity
- **Logistic Regression** --- extending to binary outcomes
- **Generalized Linear Models** --- the unifying framework

By semester's end, you'll understand that familiar tests like the *t*-test and ANOVA are simply special cases of the regression model.


## Learning Objectives

By the end of this course, you will be able to:

1. **Specify** linear and generalized linear regression models with continuous and categorical predictors, interactions, and nonlinear terms
2. **Interpret** regression coefficients, confidence intervals, and test statistics in substantive and statistical terms
3. **Diagnose** violations of model assumptions and identify influential observations
4. **Build** parsimonious models using confirmatory and exploratory strategies
5. **Extend** regression to binary and ordinal outcomes via logistic and ordinal regression
6. **Implement** all statistical analyses in R with reproducible, well-documented code
7. **Communicate** statistical findings clearly to diverse audiences


## Course Materials

All course materials are **open-access** and free. You will also need R, RStudio, and internet access.

### Textbooks and Resources

| Material | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| {{< fa book-open >}} [**Lecture Notes**]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}){target="_blank"} | Primary textbook: 13 chapters covering theory, math, and R code |
| {{< fa laptop-code >}} [**R Lab Book**]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}){target="_blank"} | 12 hands-on tutorials for applied practice |
| {{< fa clipboard-list >}} [**Project Guide**]({{< var books.project_guide >}}){target="_blank"} | Guidelines, examples, and rubrics for the course projects |
| {{< fa database >}} [**regdatasets**]({{< var books.regdatasets >}}){target="_blank"} | R package with 24 curated educational datasets |

### Software

- **R** (free) --- Download from [cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org/)
- **RStudio** (free) --- Download from [posit.co](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)
- **Posit Cloud** --- Free browser-based RStudio at [posit.cloud](https://posit.cloud/) (no installation needed)

For installation help, see the [R Setup Guide](/resource/r-setup.qmd).


## Course Structure

This is a **fully asynchronous online course**. Each week, you will:

1. {{< fa book-open >}} **Read** the Lecture Notes chapter(s)
2. {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Complete** the R Lab tutorial(s)
3. {{< fa pencil >}} **Submit** Homework or Project Milestone (when due)
4. {{< fa clipboard-check >}} **Take** the Module Quiz (when due)

See the [Schedule](/schedule.qmd) for the 15-week outline.

**Expected workload**: 10--12 hours per week for a 3-credit graduate course.


## Grading and Assessment

::: {.callout-note}
## Grading Philosophy

This course prioritizes **learning over perfection**. Homework is graded on completion (not correctness), and quizzes allow unlimited attempts. This approach reduces statistics anxiety and encourages genuine engagement with the material.

*Note: This website reflects the updated Fall 2026 assessment plan. If there are any discrepancies with earlier course documentation, this website is the authoritative source.*
:::

Your final grade is based on four components:

### Grading Breakdown

| Category | Weight | Details |
|----------|:------:|---------|
| **Homework** | 50% | 8 assignments (completion-based) |
| **Module Quizzes** | 10% | 6 quizzes (mastery-based, unlimited attempts) |
| **Project 1: Linear Regression** | 20% | Proposal + Draft + Final Report |
| **Project 2: Logistic Regression** | 20% | Proposal + Draft + Final Report |
| **Total** | **100%** | |

### Assignment Details

**Homework (50%)**: Eight assignments, approximately biweekly. Odd-numbered homework focuses on conceptual questions (hand calculations, interpretation); even-numbered focuses on computational problems (R code, data analysis). Graded for **completion**: full credit for demonstrating genuine effort, zero for non-submission. A "meaningful attempt" means:

- All problems are attempted (even if you are unsure of the answer)
- For computational assignments: R code runs without errors
- Written interpretations are included where requested

**Module Quizzes (10%)**: Six concept-check quizzes with multiple-choice and short-answer questions. **Mastery-based**: unlimited attempts, highest score counts. Open-book and open-notes. Scoring 80% or higher earns full credit.

**Project 1: Linear Regression (20%)**: A complete linear regression analysis on a dataset of your choosing.

| Component | Due | Weight |
|-----------|:---:|:------:|
| Proposal | Week 7 | 30% of Project 1 |
| Draft | Week 9 | 30% of Project 1 |
| Final Report | Week 12 | 40% of Project 1 |

**Project 2: Logistic Regression (20%)**: A complete logistic regression analysis, building on skills from Project 1.

| Component | Due | Weight |
|-----------|:---:|:------:|
| Proposal | Week 12 | 20% of Project 2 |
| Draft | Week 14 | 30% of Project 2 |
| Final Report | Week 15 | 50% of Project 2 |

See the [Project Guide]({{< var books.project_guide >}}){target="_blank"} for detailed guidelines and worked examples.

### Letter Grades

| Grade | Range | | Grade | Range |
|:-----:|:-----:|-|:-----:|:-----:|
| A | 90--100% | | C | 70--76% |
| B+ | 87--89% | | D | 60--69% |
| B | 80--86% | | F | < 60% |
| C+ | 77--79% | | | |


## Course Policies

### Late Work

- **10% per day** deducted for late submissions
- **Extensions** granted for documented hardships---email me with details
- **Absolute cutoff**: No work accepted more than **7 days late** without prior arrangement

### Academic Integrity

All work must comply with the [University of Alabama Academic Misconduct Policy](https://catalog.ua.edu/undergraduate/about/academic-regulations/student-expectations/academic-misconduct-policy/).

**Regarding AI tools** (ChatGPT, Copilot, etc.):

- You **may** use AI tools to learn concepts and debug code
- You **must** understand and adapt any AI-generated code
- You **cannot** submit entirely AI-generated work without attribution and substantial modification
- If in doubt, disclose your use of AI---transparency is never penalized

### Disability Accommodations

Students with disabilities should register with the [Office of Disability Services (ODS)](https://ods.ua.edu/) and share their accommodation letter during the first week of class.

### Religious Observances

Contact me at least one week in advance if religious observances conflict with deadlines.

### Title IX

The University of Alabama is committed to providing a safe environment. For confidential support, contact the [UA Counseling Center](https://counseling.sl.ua.edu/) at (205) 348-3863. For formal reports, contact the [Title IX Office](https://titleix.ua.edu/) at (205) 348-5496.


## Getting Help

::: {.callout-important}
## You Can Do This

Every programmer---from beginners to experts---gets stuck. It is 100% normal.

When you're stuck:

1. **Take a break.** Step away. Your subconscious will keep working.
2. **Read the error message carefully.** Google "rstats [your error]".
3. **Ask for help.** Email me or visit office hours.
4. **Review the R Lab Book.** Work through the examples step by step.

You will get unstuck. I promise.
:::

### Contact Me

- **Email**: [{{< var instructor.email >}}](mailto:{{< var instructor.email >}}) --- I aim to respond within 48 hours
- **Urgent matters**: Include "BER 640 - URGENT" in the subject line
- **Appointments**: [Schedule a meeting]({{< var instructor.appointment_url >}}) via Zoom or in-person

### Additional Support

- **Counseling Center**: [counseling.sl.ua.edu](https://counseling.sl.ua.edu/) --- free, confidential support
- **Dean of Students**: [dos.sl.ua.edu](https://dos.sl.ua.edu/) --- emergency financial assistance, food pantry, housing support
- **Writing Center**: [writingcenter.ua.edu](https://writingcenter.ua.edu/)
- **UA IT Help Desk**: [oit.ua.edu](https://oit.ua.edu/) --- (205) 348-5555

### Course Resources

Browse the [Resources](/resource/index.qmd) page for R setup guides, dataset documentation, style guides, and FAQs.
